### Mensaje:
Now I see the key difference. In `useConfirmarEntrega` and `useCancelarPedido`, the `onSuccess` callback calls:\n1. `queryClient.setQueryData(...)` to optimistically update the cache\n2. `queryClient.invalidateQueries(...)` to trigger refetch\n\nBut in the item hooks, they ONLY call `queryClient.invalidateQueries(...)` - they don't update the cache optimistically. So when the refetch fails (401), the cache still has the old data, and the UI reverts.\n\nThe fix: Add `queryClient.setQueryData` in the `onSuccess` of each item hook to update the cache with the optimistic changes, similar to the pattern in `useConfirmarEntrega`.\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`, the optimistic update would:\n- Get the current pedido from cache `['pedido', pedidoId]`\n- Filter out the deleted item from `pedido.items`\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`, the optimistic update would:\n- Get the current pedido from cache\n- Add the new item to `pedido.items`\n\nFor `useActualizarCantidadItem`, the optimistic update would:\n- Get the current pedido from cache\n- Update the item's cantidad in `pedido.items`\n\nBut wait, there's a complication. The `onSuccess` callback receives the data returned by `mutationFn`. For offline mode, we return simplified data like `{ itemId, cantidad }` or `{ itemId }`. In online mode, the request might return the full updated `Pedido` object.\n\nLet me check what the actual API returns. Looking at the service functions:\n\n```ts\nexport async function agregarItemRequest(\n  pedidoId: string,\n  data: { itemId: string; cantidad: number }\n): Promise<Pedido> {\n  const response = await apiClient.post<Pedido>(`/pedidos/${pedidoId}/items`, data);\n  return response.data;\n}\n```\n\nIt returns `Pedido` - the full updated pedido. So in success mode, `data` is the full `Pedido`. In offline mode, we return `{ itemId, cantidad }`.\n\nSo in `onSuccess`, we need to handle both cases:\n- If `data` has `items` (full Pedido) ÔåÆ use it directly\n- Otherwise ÔåÆ manually update the cache with the item change\n\nLet me plan the fix:\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  // Check if we got full pedido (API success) or optimistic data (offline)\n  if ('items' in data) {\n    // Full Pedido returned from API, invalidate to ensure consistency\n    queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  } else {\n    // Offline mode: manually update cache\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      return {\n        ...old,\n        items: [\n          ...old.items,\n          {\n            id: `optimistic-${Date.now()}`,\n            item: { id: data.itemId } as Item,\n            cantidad: data.cantidad,\n            precioUnitario: 0,\n          } as PedidoItem,\n        ],\n      };\n    });\n  }\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nHmm, but this is getting complex. Let me think about this differently.\n\nActually, looking at how `useConfirmarEntrega` works: it ALWAYS calls `setQueryData` in `onSuccess`, regardless of whether it was API or offline. The API returns the full Pedido too. But `onSuccess` receives just `data: { id, estado }` from the offline fallback. The `onSuccess` handles both cases by just checking for the fields it needs.\n\nFor item mutations, it's trickier because we need to modify the items array.\n\nActually, I think the cleanest approach is to keep it simple:\n1. In `onSuccess`, check if `data` has `items` (full Pedido from API)\n2. If yes, just use `setQueryData(data)` directly (overwrite the cached pedido)\n3. If no (offline), manually update the items array in the cache\n\nWait, even simpler: `queryClient.setQueryData` with the full pedido if it has `items`, otherwise do incremental update.\n\nLet me think about another approach. What if the `guardarCambios` function in the screen itself updated the local state/cache instead of relying on the hooks? No, that would break the architecture.\n\nOK, I think the cleanest solution is:\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n- In `onSuccess`, check if data is full Pedido (has `items` property)\n- If full Pedido ÔåÆ `queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data)` (or just invalidate)\n- If offline data ÔåÆ update cache with setQueryData to add the item\n\nSame pattern for the other two.\n\nActually, looking at the existing confirmar/cancelar hooks more carefully:\n\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', data.id], (old) => {\n    if (old) return { ...old, estado: 'ENTREGADO' as const };\n    return old;\n  });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nThey use `setQueryData` with an updater function. The updater receives the old pedido and returns a modified copy. For item hooks, we'd do:\n\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  // Always try to update cache optimistically\n  queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n    if (!old) return old;\n    // ... modify items array based on operation ...\n  });\n  // Then invalidate to get fresh data when online\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nBut the problem is that in `onSuccess`, we don't know the precise `itemId` that was passed to `mutationFn`. Wait, for `useQuitarItem`, the mutationFn receives `itemId`, but `onSuccess` receives the `data` returned from mutationFn. In offline mode we return `{ itemId }`, which has the info. In online mode, we'd get the full Pedido.\n\nActually, hold on. Let me re-check what happens in online mode. If the API succeeds, `agregarItemRequest` returns `response.data` which is the full `Pedido` (with updated items). So `onSuccess(data)` receives `Pedido`. The `Pedido` type has `items`, so we could just replace the entire cached pedido.\n\nBut wait - `queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', data.id], data)` would work IF `data` has the full Pedido. In offline mode, `data` is just `{ itemId, cantidad }` which doesn't have `items` or `id` (pedidoId). So we'd need to check.\n\nActually, for consistency, let me think about what the offline fallback should return. For `useCancelarPedido`, the offline response is:\n```ts\nreturn {\n  id: pedidoId,\n  estado: 'NO_ENTREGADO' as const,\n  motivoFalla: motivo,\n  actualizadoEn: new Date().toISOString(),\n};\n```\n\nThen `onSuccess` uses `data.id` to find the query, and `data.estado` and `data.motivoFalla` to update it.\n\nFor items, I should do something similar. The offline response could include `pedidoId` so we can use it in `onSuccess`:\n\nFor `useAgregarItem` (offline response):\n```ts\nreturn { pedidoId, itemId: data.itemId, cantidad: data.cantidad };\n```\n\nThen in `onSuccess`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  const pid = 'pedidoId' in data ? data.pedidoId : (data as Pedido).id;\n  \n  if ('items' in data) {\n    // Full Pedido from API\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pid], data);\n  } else {\n    // Offline: update cache manually\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pid], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      return {\n        ...old,\n        items: [\n          ...old.items,\n          { id: `opt-${Date.now()}`, item: { id: data.itemId } as Item, cantidad: data.cantidad, precioUnitario: 0 } as PedidoItem,\n        ],\n      };\n    });\n  }\n  \n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n};\n```\n\nThis is getting quite complex. Let me consider a simpler approach.\n\nActually, the simplest fix is to just replace `onSuccess` with `onSettled` and add `setQueryData` with something that always works.\n\nWait, actually the simplest approach is: ALWAYS invalidate (to trigger refetch), but also ALWAYS setQueryData with the optimistic update. The invalidation will try to refetch. If it succeeds, the server data overwrites our optimistic data. If it fails, the optimistic data remains.\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n    if (!old) return old;\n    return {\n      ...old,\n      items: [...old.items, { /* temp optimistic item */ } as PedidoItem],\n    };\n  });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nBut we need to know what to add. The `data` from mutationFn returns either:\n- Full Pedido: `{ id, items, ... }` ÔåÉ we could use this directly\n- Optimistic: `{ itemId, cantidad }` ÔåÉ we need to construct a PedidoItem\n\nHmm, this is getting messy. Let me step back and think about what the user's actual problem is more carefully.\n\nThe user says \"el boton guardar cambios sigue sin hacer nada. Por lo menos ahora si me recalcula el precio.\"\n\nTranslation: \"The save changes button still does nothing. At least now it recalculates the price.\"\n\nThe price recalculation works because the local `totalEdit` state updates when the user changes items via the stepper. This is pure local state - no API calls involved.\n\nThe \"does nothing\" likely means:\n1. After clicking \"Guardar cambios\" and confirming the Alert\n2. The button shows \"Guardando...\" briefly\n3. Then returns to \"Guardar cambios\"\n4. But the items on screen don't change\n5. And maybe no toast appears\n\nThis happens because:\n1. The mutations are called, they queue offline, return optimistic data\n2. The `showToast` and `setModoEdicion(false)` run\n3. BUT the mutation's `onSuccess` fires ÔåÆ invalidates `['pedido', pedidoId]`\n4. The refetch tries to get fresh data from API ÔåÆ fails (401)\n5. The query cache returns the OLD cached data (before item changes)\n6. The UI renders the old data\n7. The user sees the items \"restored\" to their original state\n8. If the toast was shown, it might disappear quickly or not be noticed\n\nAlso, if the user doesn't have a Toast component set up at the app level, the toast might not show at all. Let me check...\n\nActually, for the toast, looking at `useToastStore`, it's a zustand store. The UI must have a `<ToastProvider>` or `<Toaster>` component that renders based on the store state. If that's not set up, the toast message is stored but not displayed.\n\nLet me check if there's a Toast component in the app layout.

### Mensaje:
Encontr├® el problema. Las mutaciones de items NO hacen `setQueryData` (actualizaci├│n optimista del cach├®), solo invalidan. Cuando la API falla (401 del mock JWT), la invalidaci├│n dispara un refetch que tambi├®n falla, y TanStack Query restaura los **datos viejos del cach├®**. Por eso el usuario ve que \"no pas├│ nada\".\n\nCompar├í con `useConfirmarEntrega` que S├ì usa `setQueryData`:

### Mensaje:
The Toast component IS rendered in `_layout.tsx` at line 81, so toasts should work.\n\nNow I understand the problem clearly. The item mutations have `onSuccess` that only calls `invalidateQueries`, but they don't call `setQueryData`. So when the API fails (mock JWT 401), the invalidation triggers a refetch which also fails, and the cache falls back to the old data.\n\nThe fix is to add `setQueryData` in the `onSuccess` of each item hook, similar to `useConfirmarEntrega` and `useCancelarPedido`.\n\nBut the tricky part is constructing the optimistic data for items. The `mutateAsync` calls in `guardarCambios` use variables that aren't available in `onSuccess`. Let me think about how to handle this...\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`:\n- `mutateAsync(itemId)` ÔåÆ `mutationFn(itemId)` ÔåÆ API fails ÔåÆ returns `{ itemId }`\n- In `onSuccess(data)`, `data` is `{ itemId }` (offline) or `Pedido` (online)\n- If `data` has `items` ÔåÆ it's a full Pedido, just use `setQueryData` with the full object\n- If `data` has `itemId` ÔåÆ it's offline data, we need to filter the item from cache\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n- `mutateAsync({ itemId, cantidad })` ÔåÆ `mutationFn(data)` ÔåÆ API fails ÔåÆ returns `{ itemId, cantidad }`\n- In `onSuccess(data)`, `data` is `{ itemId, cantidad }` (offline) or `Pedido` (online)\n- If `data` has `items` ÔåÆ full Pedido\n- If `data` has `itemId` ÔåÆ offline, add item to cache\n\nFor `useActualizarCantidadItem`:\n- `mutateAsync({ itemId, cantidad })` ÔåÆ `mutationFn({ itemId, cantidad })` ÔåÆ API fails ÔåÆ returns `{ itemId, cantidad }`\n- Same pattern\n\nLet me implement this. The key is to check if `data` has `items` (full Pedido) vs being just an offline response.\n\nWait, but there's a typing issue. The `onSuccess` callback receives the return type of `mutationFn`. Since we return either `Pedido` or `{ itemId: string; cantidad: number }`, we need to handle both.\n\nActually, looking at the confirmar/cancelar hooks, they return a partial object from the offline fallback:\n```ts\nreturn {\n  id: pedidoId,\n  estado: 'ENTREGADO' as const,\n  actualizadoEn: new Date().toISOString(),\n};\n```\n\nAnd the `onSuccess` uses `data.id` which works for both the API response (which has `id`) and the offline response (which also has `id`).\n\nFor item hooks, I need to make sure the offline response includes enough info for the `onSuccess` to work.\n\nLet me design the fix:\n\n**useAgregarItem:**\n```ts\noffline return: { type: 'offline', pedidoId, itemId: data.itemId, cantidad: data.cantidad }\nonline return: Pedido (full object with items)\n\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  if ('items' in data) {\n    // Full Pedido from API\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data);\n  } else {\n    // Offline fallback\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      return {\n        ...old,\n        items: [\n          ...old.items,\n          { id: `offline-${Date.now()}`, item: { id: data.itemId } as Item, cantidad: data.cantidad, precioUnitario: 0 } as PedidoItem,\n        ],\n      };\n    });\n  }\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n}\n```\n\nHmm, but `PedidoItem` has more fields. Let me check the type...\n\nActually, let me think about this differently. The `PedidoItem` type:\n\nActually, I should just check what types are used. The offline fallback doesn't need to perfectly construct a PedidoItem - it just needs something that won't crash the UI. Since the UI renders `item.item.nombre`, `item.cantidad`, `item.precioUnitario`, we need at least those.\n\nBut for the offline case where the item is being added from the modal, we already have the PedidoItem object in `itemsEdit` in the screen. The problem is that the screen doesn't pass it to the mutationFn, it only passes `{ itemId: item.item.id, cantidad: item.cantidad }`.\n\nLet me think about a simpler approach...\n\nActually, the simplest fix that avoids complex type handling is to change `onSuccess` to `onSettled` and simply do nothing extra ÔÇö just let the invalidate handle it. But that's the current behavior and it doesn't work.\n\nWait, actually the issue is that `usePedidoDetalle` has a fallback to mock data when API fails:\n```ts\nexport function usePedidoDetalle(id: string) {\n  return useQuery<Pedido>({\n    queryKey: ['pedido', id],\n    queryFn: async () => {\n      try {\n        return await getPedidoByIdRequest(id);\n      } catch {\n        return await mockGetPedidoByIdRequest(id);\n      }\n    },\n    enabled: !!id,\n  });\n}\n```\n\nSo when invalidate triggers a refetch, the API call fails (401), BUT then the mock fallback kicks in! So the refetch should return mock data, not the old cached data. Unless the mock data is different from the cached data...\n\nWait, does `staleTime` affect this? If the query is `staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000` and the data is still fresh, then `invalidateQueries` would mark it as stale and trigger a refetch. The best approach would be:\n\n1. **Short-term fix**: Add `setQueryData` to item mutations so changes appear immediately in cache even if refetch fails\n2. **Long-term fix**: Get the backend working with a real JWT so API calls succeed\n\nLet me focus on the short-term fix. I need to modify the 3 item hooks to:\n1. Return `pedidoId` in the offline fallback response (so onSuccess knows which query to update)\n2. Add `setQueryData` in `onSuccess` to update the cache\n\nBut there's a complication with `useAgregarItem` and `useActualizarCantidadItem` - their onSuccess needs to know `pedidoId` to update the query key. Currently `pedidoId` is captured in the closure, which is fine.\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`, the mutationFn receives `itemId: string` and onSuccess needs to know which query key to update. `pedidoId` is in the closure, so that's fine.\n\nLet me also consider: what if the API succeeds and returns full Pedido? Then `setQueryData` with the full data would overwrite the cache correctly.\n\nOK here's my plan:\n\n**For `useAgregarItem`:**\n\n```ts\nexport function useAgregarItem(pedidoId: string) {\n  const queryClient = useQueryClient();\n  const addToQueue = useOfflineStore((s) => s.addToQueue);\n\n  return useMutation({\n    mutationFn: async (data: { itemId: string; cantidad: number }) => {\n      try {\n        return await agregarItemRequest(pedidoId, data);\n      } catch {\n        addToQueue({ type: 'AGREGAR_ITEM', payload: { pedidoId, ...data } });\n        return { _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId: data.itemId, cantidad: data.cantidad };\n      }\n    },\n    onSuccess: (data) => {\n      if ('_offline' in data) {\n        // Offline fallback: update cache optimistically\n        queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n          if (!old) return old;\n          return {\n            ...old,\n            items: [...old.items, { id: `offline-${Date.now()}`, item: { id: data.itemId } as Item, cantidad: data.cantidad, precioUnitario: 0 } as PedidoItem],\n          };\n        });\n      }\n      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n    },\n  });\n}\n```\n\nWait, but there's a typing issue. `data` is `unknown` basically because the mutationFn can return either type. The `useMutation` call infers the return type from `mutationFn`. The offline branch returns `{ id, estado, actualizadoEn }`. Since both have `id`, `data.id` works.\n\nSo the key is: the offline response needs to share enough shape with the API response for `onSuccess` to work.\n\nFor item mutations, I should use the same pattern. But since the API returns `Pedido` (full object with items), and the offline response needs to be different, I need a way for `onSuccess` to distinguish.\n\nThe simplest approach: always return the same minimal shape from both branches.\n\nWait, there's an even simpler approach. I can completely restructure the `onSuccess`:\n\nFor **useAgregarItem**:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  const fullPedido = 'items' in data ? data : null;\n  if (fullPedido) {\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], fullPedido);\n  } else {\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      return {\n        ...old,\n        items: [...old.items, {\n          id: `temp-${Date.now()}`,\n          item: { id: data.itemId },\n          cantidad: data.cantidad,\n          precioUnitario: 0,\n        } as PedidoItem],\n      };\n    });\n  }\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nHmm but there's an issue with `'items' in data`. Since `Pedido` has `items` but the offline response `{ _offline, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` doesn't have `items`, this check should work. But it's fragile because `key in obj` in TypeScript is a type guard only if the type is a union.\n\nActually, I just realized: the `data` parameter in `onSuccess` is typed based on what `mutationFn` returns. Since `mutationFn` returns `Promise<Pedido | { itemId: string; cantidad: string; _offline: boolean }>`, the `data` in `onSuccess` would be `Pedido | { itemId: string; cantidad: number; _offline: boolean }`. The offline response doesn't have `items`. ### Mensaje:
The toast store looks fine - it should show toasts for 3 seconds by default. So the toast SHOULD appear... unless the mutated data from the store doesn't trigger a re-render of the Toast component, or the Toast component has a bug.\n\nActually, looking at `_layout.tsx` line 81, the `<Toast />` component is rendered. Let me check the Toast component to make sure it reads from the store correctly.\n\nBut actually, let me focus on the actual fix. The main problem is that after the mutations execute (with offline fallback), the `onSuccess` invalidates the query, which triggers a refetch. The refetch fails (401), and...\n\nWait, actually I need to think about this more carefully. The `usePedidoDetalle` queryFn has a try/catch with mock fallback:\n\n```ts\nqueryFn: async () => {\n  try {\n    return await getPedidoByIdRequest(id);\n  } catch {\n    return await mockGetPedidoByIdRequest(id);\n  }\n},\n```\n\nSo when the refetch happens, the API call fails (401) ÔåÆ falls back to mock data ÔåÆ returns mock data. The mock data would be a \"generic\" pedido, not the specific one with the user's edits.\n\nBut the toast DOES show \"Items actualizados correctamente\" before the refetch happens. So the user should see a success toast, then the screen refreshes with mock data.\n\nHmm, wait. `setModoEdicion(false)` runs after all mutations succeed. Then React re-renders with... what? With `pedido` from `usePedidoDetalle(id)`. Since `onSuccess` invalidated the query, the mutation's success triggers a refetch. But the refetch is async. So:\n\n1. `setModoEdicion(false)` ÔåÆ re-render, shows pedido data (from cache, which is the LAST successful fetch = original data)\n2. Refetch starts ÔåÆ fails ÔåÆ returns mock data ÔåÆ updates cache ÔåÆ re-render again with mock data\n\nSo the user would see:\n1. Success toast\n2. Edit mode exits ÔåÆ original data shown (from cache)\n3. A moment later ÔåÆ data refreshes to mock data (if it's different)\n\nOr if the mock data is the same as the original data, step 2 and 3 look the same.\n\nThe user says \"sigue sin hacer nada\" which could mean:\n- They don't see a toast (maybe they dismiss it or miss it)\n- The items go back to their original state\n- They expected to see the items changed but they revert\n\nActually wait, let me look more carefully. `usePedidoDetalle` uses `useQuery` with `staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000`. When `invalidateQueries` is called, it marks the query as stale. Then any component using that query will trigger a refetch. But the refetch is NOT synchronous - it happens on the next render.\n\nSo the timeline is:\n1. MutateAsync resolves (offline fallback)\n2. showToast('Items actualizados correctamente', 'success')\n3. setModoEdicion(false) ÔåÆ re-render with old pedido data\n4. setItemsEdit([])\n5. setGuardando(false)\n6. Mutation hooks' onSuccess fires ÔåÆ invalidateQueries(['pedido', id])\n7. React re-renders... `usePedidoDetalle` sees the query is stale ÔåÆ triggers refetch\n8. Refetch tries API ÔåÆ fails ÔåÆ returns mock data\n9. Cache updates with mock data\n10. React re-renders with mock data\n\nBetween steps 3 and 10, the user briefly sees old data, then mock data. If the mock data has the same items, it looks like nothing changed.\n\nSo the real fix is to add `setQueryData` in the `onSuccess` of each item mutation to update the cache with the changes BEFORE invalidating. This way:\n1. Cache is updated with the changes immediately\n2. Invalidation triggers refetch\n3. If refetch succeeds ÔåÆ server data overwrites cache ÔåÆ correct items\n4. If refetch fails ÔåÆ cache still has the optimistic updates ÔåÆ user sees changes\n\nLet me now design the implementation. I need to modify the 3 item hooks with `setQueryData` logic.\n\nFor **useAgregarItem**:\n- `onSuccess` receives `data` which is either `Pedido` (API success) or `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` (offline)\n- If full Pedido ÔåÆ `setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data)`\n- If offline ÔåÆ `setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], old => modify items)`\n- Then invalidate\n\nFor **useQuitarItem**:\n- `onSuccess` receives `data` which is either `Pedido` (API success) or `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId }` (offline)\n- If full Pedido ÔåÆ `setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data)`\n- If offline ÔåÆ `setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], old => filter out item)`\n\nFor **useActualizarCantidadItem**:\n- `onSuccess` receives `data` which is either `Pedido` (API success) or `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` (offline)\n- If full Pedido ÔåÆ `setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data)`\n- If offline ÔåÆ `setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], old => update item cantidad)`\n\nActually, I just realized there might be a simpler approach. Instead of using `_offline` flags, I can use the fact that `Pedido` has an `items` property of type `PedidoItem[]`. The offline response won't have `items`. So:\n\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  if ('items' in data) {\n    // Full Pedido from API\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data);\n  } else {\n    // Offline fallback: update cache manually\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      // ... modify items\n    });\n  }\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nWait, but there's a TypeScript issue. If `mutationFn` returns `Pedido | { _offline: boolean, ... }`, then `data` in `onSuccess` is `Pedido | { _offline: boolean, ... }`. And `'items' in data` would work as a type guard if only `Pedido` has `items`.\n\nActually, do I even need the union? Let me think about another approach: always return a consistent shape from mutationFn.\n\nWhat if the offline fallback returns `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` and I check for `_offline` in `onSuccess`? That's cleaner.\n\nBut I also need `Item` and `PedidoItem` types for the `setQueryData` updater. Let me import them.\n\nOK let me plan the exact edits:\n\n### 1. `usePedidos.ts` - imports\nAdd:\n```ts\nimport type { Item, PedidoItem } from '@/types/item';\n```\n\n### 2. `useAgregarItem` - modify offline return and onSuccess\nOffline return: `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }`\nonSuccess: check `_offline`, update cache accordingly\n\n### 3. `useQuitarItem` - same pattern\nOffline return: `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId }`\nonSuccess: check `_offline`, filter out item\n\n### 4. `useActualizarCantidadItem` - same pattern\nOffline return: `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }`\nonSuccess: check `_offline`, update cantidad\n\n### 5. Tests - update tests\nThe tests check that `addToQueue` was called with correct payload and that the result matches expected. The tests need to be updated if the offline return type changes.\n\nLet me check what the current tests expect...\n\nLooking at the test I added:\n```ts\nit('encola en offlineStore cuando agregarItem falla', async () => {\n  mockAgregarItem.mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error('Network Error'));\n  // ...\n  const result = await opts.mutationFn(payload);\n  expect(mockAddToQueue).toHaveBeenCalledWith({\n    type: 'AGREGAR_ITEM',\n    payload: { pedidoId: 'p1', itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 },\n  });\n  expect(result).toEqual({ itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 });\n});\n```\n\nIf I change the offline return to `{ _offline: true, pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }`, I need to update the test to expect the new return shape.\n\nActually wait, maybe I should NOT add the `_offline: true` flag. Let me reconsider.\n\nThe full `Pedido` returned from the API has `items: PedidoItem[]`. The offline fallback returns `{ pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` (no `items`). So I can check `'items' in data` to distinguish.\n\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  if ('items' in data) {\n    // Full Pedido from API\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], data);\n  } else {\n    // Offline\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      // modify items...\n    });\n  }\n  // invalidate...\n}\n```\n\nBut there's a TypeScript issue. `'items' in data` narrows `data` based on whether the property exists. For this to work as a type guard, `data` must be a union type where one member has `items` and others don't. Since `mutationFn` returns `Pedido | { pedidoId: string, itemId: string, cantidad: number }`, this should work.\n\nLet me verify: `Pedido` has `items: PedidoItem[]`. The offline response `{ pedidoId, itemId, cantidad }` does NOT have `items`. So `'items' in data` will narrow `data` to `Pedido` when true, and to the offline type when false. \n\nOK, BUT there's another approach which is simpler: just always update via setQueryData without checking. If the mutation succeeded (API), the invalidation will refetch and overwrite anyway. The setQueryData is \"optimistic\" - it shows data immediately while the refetch is in flight.\n\nActually, for the offline fallback, we MUST update the cache manually because the refetch will fail. For API success, we DON'T need to update manually because the refetch will bring fresh data. But it's harmless to also update - it just makes the UI respond faster.\n\nSo I can simplify to ALWAYS do `setQueryData` + `invalidateQueries`:\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  if (!('items' in data)) {\n    // Offline: manually update cache\n    queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n      if (!old) return old;\n      return {\n        ...old,\n        items: [...old.items, { id: `offline-${Date.now()}`, item: { id: data.itemId } as Item, cantidad: data.cantidad, precioUnitario: 0 } as PedidoItem],\n      };\n    });\n  }\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nActually, I realize there's a problem with this approach. The \"Item\" type has more than just `id`. When constructing the PedidoItem for the offline cache update, I need at minimum `item.nombre` for rendering. But the offline response only has `itemId`.\n\nLooking at how the UI renders a PedidoItem:\n```tsx\n<Text style={[styles.itemNombre, { color: theme.text }]}>\n  {item.item.nombre}\n</Text>\n```\n\nSo we need `item.nombre`. But we don't have it in the offline fallback.\n\nHowever, when the cache already has the pedido (from initial load), `items` are already populated with full item objects. When we add a new item (agregarItem), the item isn't in the cache yet - it's a new item.\n\nSo this is a problem. For agregarItem offline, we'd need the full item data to construct a PedidoItem. But we only have `itemId`.\n\nHmm, let me think about alternatives:\n\n1. **Pass the full PedidoItem in mutateAsync**: Change `mutateAsync({ itemId, cantidad })` to `mutateAsync(pedidoItem)` where pedidoItem has the full item data. But that's a bigger change.\n\n2. **Don't try to reconstruct PedidoItem**: Instead, just add a placeholder in the cache and let the user know the item will appear when synced.\n\n3. **Use a different approach entirely**: Instead of updating TanStack Query cache, just rely on the `modoEdicion` local state. But `setModoEdicion(false)` would exit edit mode and show TanStack Query data.\n\n4. **Keep `modoEdicion` as true until sync completes**: Don't exit edit mode until the queue is processed. But that's complex.\n\nActually, the simplest fix for the short term is to change the `guardarCambios` function in `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx` to NOT exit edit mode when mutations queue offline. Instead, it should keep the edit state and show a \"pendiente de sincronizar\" indicator.\n\nBut that's a larger change to the screen.\n\nLet me think about what the user actually expects. They said \"el boton guardar cambios sigue sin hacer nada.\" They expect changes to be saved and the UI to update. The best UX would be:\n\n1. Press \"Guardar cambios\"\n2. If online: API calls work, success toast, exit edit mode, show updated data\n3. If offline: queue mutations, show \"Cambios guardados localmente\" toast, exit edit mode, show updated items (even if mock/placeholder)\n\nFor case 3 to work, I need to update the TanStack Query cache with the modified items. But I need enough data to render.\n\nLet me take yet another approach. Instead of trying to reconstruct PedidoItem in the hook, I'll pass the FULL items array (before and after) through the mutation somehow.\n\nActually, the simplest fix might be in `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`: in `guardarCambios`, before calling the mutations, save the `itemsEdit` state. Then after all mutations complete, update the pedido data in the cache with the edited items.\n\nI can do this by calling `queryClient.setQueryData` directly in the screen:\n\n```ts\nconst guardarCambios = useCallback(() => {\n  if (!pedido) return;\n  Alert.alert(..., async () => {\n    setGuardando(true);\n    try {\n      // ... run mutations ...\n      \n      // Optimistically update cache with edited items\n      const pedidoActualizado = {\n        ...pedido,\n        items: itemsEdit,\n      };\n      queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', id], pedidoActualizado);\n      \n      showToast('Items actualizados correctamente', 'success');\n      setModoEdicion(false);\n      setItemsEdit([]);\n    } catch (err) {\n      // ...\n    } finally {\n      setGuardando(false);\n    }\n  });\n}, [pedido, itemsEdit, id, ...]);\n```\n\nThis is much simpler! The screen already has `pedido` (from cache) and `itemsEdit` (the modified items). After mutations, it can directly update the cache with the modified pedido. Then when edit mode exits, the UI shows the updated items from the cache.\n\nAnd when the mutation refetch eventually succeeds (when online), the real data from the server will overwrite the optimistic update.\n\nThis is a much better approach. Let me also add `useQueryClient` to the screen's imports.\n\nWait, `useQueryClient` is not currently imported in the screen. I need to add it. And I need to get the `queryClient` in the `guardarCambios` callback.\n\nBut wait, `guardarCambios` is wrapped in `useCallback`. I would need to add `queryClient` to the dependency array. Since `queryClient` from `useQueryClient()` is stable, this shouldn't cause re-creation issues.\n\nLet me also think about whether this is the RIGHT solution or if there's something simpler. Actually, there IS a simpler approach:\n\nIn `guardarCambios`, after all mutations run (successfully or queued offline), DON'T exit edit mode with `setModoEdicion(false)`. Instead, keep `modoEdicion = true` but mark the changes as saved by clearing the dirty state.\n\nWait no, that doesn't work either. The user expects to exit edit mode and see the changes.\n\nOK I think the `queryClient.setQueryData` approach in the screen is the cleanest. Let me present this plan to the user.\n\nActually, wait. Let me reconsider. There's an even simpler approach that fixes the real issue more elegantly:\n\nThe `onSuccess` of each mutation currently calls `invalidateQueries(['pedido', pedidoId])`. The problem is that when refetch fails, data reverts. But what if I also call `setQueryData` in each mutation's `onSuccess`?\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: (data) => {\n  queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n    if (!old) return old;\n    const itemId = 'items' in data ? /* extract from items */ : data.itemId;\n    return {\n      ...old,\n      items: old.items.filter(i => i.id !== itemId),\n      total: old.items.filter(i => i.id !== itemId).reduce(...)\n    };\n  });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n}\n```\n\nThis way, the cache is updated immediately. The refetch happens asynchronously. If it fails, the optimistic data stays. If it succeeds, the server data overwrites.\n\nBut we still have the issue of constructing PedidoItem for agregarItem. For the screen's UX, even a partial item would be OK temporarily.\n\nActually, let me just go with the screen-level approach since it has access to the full `itemsEdit` state. Let me present the plan to the user.\n\nLet me verify my plan by re-reading the `guardarCambios` function and the screen to ensure I know what imports to add.\n\nCurrent imports in PedidoDetalleScreen:\n```ts\nimport { useState, useCallback } from 'react';\nimport { Alert, Linking, Modal, Platform, ScrollView, StyleSheet, Text, TextInput, TouchableOpacity, View } from 'react-native';\nimport { router } from 'expo-router';\nimport { ThemedView } from '@/components/themed-view';\nimport { Card, Button, LoadingSpinner, ErrorMessage, Header } from '@/components/ui';\nimport { Colors, Spacing, FontSizes, BorderRadius } from '@/constants/theme';\nimport { useColorScheme } from '@/hooks/use-color-scheme';\nimport { usePedidoDetalle, useConfirmarEntrega, useCancelarPedido, useQuitarItem, useAgregarItem, useActualizarCantidadItem } from '@/features/pedidos/hooks/usePedidos';\nimport { useToast } from '@/hooks/useToast';\nimport { handleApiError } from '@/services/handleApiError';\nimport { getItemsRequest } from '@/services/items';\nimport { MOCK_MOTIVOS } from '@/mocks/mockData';\nimport type { EstadoPedido, MotivoCancelacion, Pedido } from '@/types';\nimport type { Item } from '@/types/item';\nimport type { PedidoItem } from '@/types/item';\n```\n\n`useQueryClient` is not imported. I would add:\n```ts\nimport { useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';\n```\n\nThen in the component, I'd add:\n```ts\nconst queryClient = useQueryClient();\n```\n\nAnd in `guardarCambios`, before `showToast`:\n```ts\n// Optimistic cache update\nqueryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', id], (old) => {\n  if (!old) return old;\n  return { ...old, items: itemsEdit };\n});\n```\n\nThis is clean and uses the data the screen already has. Let me present this plan.

### Mensaje:
Now I have a complete understanding. Let me present the plan to the user.\n\nThe problem:\n1. Item mutations (`useAgregarItem`, `useQuitarItem`, `useActualizarCantidadItem`) only call `invalidateQueries` in `onSuccess`, which triggers a refetch\n2. The refetch tries API ÔåÆ fails (mock JWT 401) ÔåÆ falls back to mock data\n3. The mock data doesn't have the user's edits\n4. So the UI reverts to the original/unchanged data\n5. User thinks \"no hizo nada\"\n\nThe solution: Add `queryClient.setQueryData` in `guardarCambios` (in `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`) to optimistically update the cache with the edited items BEFORE the refetch. This way:\n- Cache is updated immediately with the user's changes\n- Refetch happens in background\n- If refetch succeeds ÔåÆ server data overwrites cache\n- If refetch fails ÔåÆ cache keeps the optimistic data ÔåÆ user sees changes\n\nThis approach is simpler than modifying each hook because:\n- The screen already has `itemsEdit` (the modified items)\n- No need to reconstruct PedidoItem objects from partial data\n- Single point of change\n\nLet me present this as a clear plan.

### Mensaje:
## Diagn├│stico completo\n\nEncontr├® **dos problemas** que explican por qu├® \"no hace nada\":\n\n### Problema 1 (principal): El cach├® se revierte\n\nLas mutaciones de items (`useAgregarItem`, `useQuitarItem`, `useActualizarCantidadItem`) solo llaman `invalidateQueries` en `onSuccess`. El flujo actual es:\n\n```\nguardarCambios ÔåÆ mutateAsync (offline ÔåÆ ok) ÔåÆ setModoEdicion(false)\n  ÔåÆ onSuccess ÔåÆ invalidateQueries ÔåÆ refetch ÔåÆ API falla (401)\n  ÔåÆ mockGetPedidoByIdRequest ÔåÆ datos mock (sin edits)\n  ÔåÆ UI muestra datos originales\n```\n\nEl usuario ve que los items **vuelven a su estado original** porque el refetch trae datos mock. Las mutaciones `useConfirmarEntrega` y `useCancelarPedido** evitan esto porque S├ì hacen `setQueryData` en `onSuccess`.\n\n### Problema 2: El Toast se muestra pero tal vez no se ve\n\nNo es un bug pero puede contribuir a la percepci├│n de \"no pas├│ nada\". El toast se muestra pero la UI se refresca inmediatamente despu├®s con datos viejos, dando la sensaci├│n de que no hubo cambio.\n\n---\n\n## Plan de soluci├│n\n\nPropongo arreglarlo desde **`PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`**, que ya tiene toda la data necesaria (`itemsEdit`), en lugar de modificar los 3 hooks:\n\n### Cambio ├║nico en `guardarCambios`\n\nAgregar `queryClient.setQueryData` con los items editados ANTES de salir del modo edici├│n:\n\n```ts\n// En guardarCambios, despu├®s de las mutaciones y antes de showToast:\n\n// Actualizaci├│n optimista del cach├®\nqueryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', id], (old) => {\n  if (!old) return old;\n  return { ...old, items: itemsEdit };\n});\n\nshowToast('Items actualizados correctamente', 'success');\nsetModoEdicion(false);\nsetItemsEdit([]);\n```\n\n### Archivos a modificar\n\n| Archivo | Cambio |\n|---|---|\n| `features/pedidos/screens/PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx` | Agregar `import { useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query'` y llamar `queryClient.setQueryData` en `guardarCambios` |\n\n### Por qu├® esta soluci├│n es mejor\n\n1. **Simple**: Un solo cambio localizado, no toca los 3 hooks ni los tests\n2. **Correcto**: `itemsEdit` tiene los items completos (con `item.nombre`, `item.precio`, etc.) para construir el `Pedido` actualizado correctamente\n3. **No destructivo**: La invalidaci├│n sigue ocurriendo. Si el backend funciona, los datos del servidor sobrescriben el cache. Si no, el cache mantiene los cambios del usuario\n4. **Consistente**: El usuario ve exactamente lo que edit├│, no datos mock\n\n### Riesgos\n\n- Bajo: `queryClient` es estable (no causa re-renders extra)\n- Si hay muchas mutaciones encoladas offline y el usuario recarga la app, los cambios no persistir├ín hasta que `useOfflineSync` los procese (comportamiento esperado)\n\n---\n\n**┬┐Aplico este plan?** Es un cambio m├¡nimo que deber├¡a hacer que \"Guardar cambios\" funcione visiblemente: los items muestran los cambios, el toast aparece, y el modo edici├│n se cierra correctamente.

## Ô£à Aplicado\n\nCambios realizados en `features/pedidos/screens/PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`:\n\n1. **Import**: `import { useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query'` (l├¡nea 15)\n2. **Hook**: `const queryClient = useQueryClient();` (l├¡nea 102)\n3. **Cache update**: En `guardarCambios`, justo antes del toast (l├¡neas 277-282):\n   ```ts\n   queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', id], (old) => {\n     if (!old) return old;\n     return { ...old, items: itemsEdit };\n   });\n   ```\n\n### Qu├® cambia\n\n- **Antes**: Las mutaciones se encolaban offline ÔåÆ `onSuccess` invalidaba la query ÔåÆ refetch fallaba (401) ÔåÆ TanStack Query devolv├¡a los **datos viejos del cach├®** ÔåÆ el usuario ve├¡a que \"no pas├│ nada\"\n- **Ahora**: Las mutaciones se encolan offline ÔåÆ actualizamos el cach├® con `itemsEdit` (los items que el usuario edit├│) ÔåÆ toast \"Items actualizados correctamente\" ÔåÆ sale del modo edici├│n ÔåÆ la UI muestra **los items editados** ÔåÆ el refetch en background falla, pero el cach├® ya tiene los datos correctos\n\nEl bot├│n **\"Guardar cambios\"** ahora deber├¡a mostrar los cambios en pantalla aunque el backend rechace el mock JWT. Los cambios se sincronizar├ín con el servidor cuando `useOfflineSync` procese la cola (cada 30s).

'Guardando...' : 'Guardar cambios'}\n      variant=\"primary\"\n      onPress={guardarCambios}\n      disabled={guardando}\n    />\n```\n\nIf `modoEdicion` is true and `guardando` is false, the button should show and be tappable. But `TouchableOpacity` receives `onPress` as a prop, it doesn't memoize it. So even if the reference changes, React re-renders the button with the new onPress.\n\nUnless... `Button` is somehow memoized? Let me check:\n\n```tsx\nexport default function Button({\n  title,\n  loading = false,\n  style,\n  disabled,\n  ...rest\n}: ButtonProps) {\n```\n\nNo, `Button` is not wrapped in `React.memo`, so it re-renders whenever parent re-renders.\n\nHmm, let me think about \"sigue sin hacer nada\" again. What if the issue is that the Alert appears BUT the \"Confirmar\" callback doesn't execute properly because of async issues?\n\nIn React Native, `Alert.alert` button `onPress` callbacks are synchronous in nature. When you pass an async function, the promise is just not awaited. But the function should still execute synchronously up to the first `await`.\n\n```ts\n{ text: 'Confirmar', onPress: async () => {\n  setGuardando(true);  // This runs synchronously\n  try {\n    // ...\n    for (const itemId of deletedIds) {\n      await quitarItemMutation.mutateAsync(itemId);  // First await - async\n    }\n    // ... more awaits\n  }\n}}\n```\n\nThe `setGuardando(true)` runs synchronously. Then the first `await` happens. After that, the rest runs asynchronously. This should work fine.\n\nWait, let me check if there's an issue with the `itemsEdit` state. When the user is in edit mode and modifies items, `itemsEdit` changes via `handleCambiarCantidad` and `handleEliminarItem`. These use `setItemsEdit` with a functional updater, so they should always have the latest state.\n\nBut `guardarCambios` is wrapped in `useCallback` with `itemsEdit` in the dependency array. So when `itemsEdit` changes, `guardarCambios` gets a new reference. The button re-renders with the new `onPress`. This should work.\n\nLet me think about this from a different angle. Could the issue be that `pedido` in the closure is undefined or stale?\n\n```ts\nconst guardarCambios = useCallback(() => {\n  if (!pedido) return;  // Guard clause - if pedido is null, do nothing\n  // ...\n}, [pedido, itemsEdit, id, showToast, quitarItemMutation, agregarItemMutation, actualizarCantidadMutation, queryClient]);\n```\n\nIf `usePedidoDetalle(id)` returns `{ data: undefined, isLoading: true }` or if the data is being refetched, `pedido` could be undefined. But in edit mode, we've already rendered the detail view, so `pedido` should be defined.\n\nOK, I think I need to try a different approach. Let me check if there's an issue with the way the mutations are set up. The mutations now have the offline fallback that catches errors. But what if the mutations are hanging instead of rejecting?\n\nLet me look at the API client timeout:\n\nFrom `services/api.ts`, there's a timeout setting. If the backend is NOT running, the Axios request would take a while to timeout (default 1000ms? Let me check).\n\nActually, I checked earlier - the user said the mock JWT causes 401 errors. So the backend IS running but the JWT is rejected. The 401 response should be quick.\n\nBut wait - there's the response interceptor in `apiClient` that handles 401:\n\nFrom line 43 of `services/api.ts`: \"apiClient response interceptor skips auto-logout for the mock token (line 43) but still rejects the promise.\"\n\nSo the promise rejects with the 401 error, which IS caught by our try/catch in the mutationFn. Good.\n\nHmm, let me re-read what the user said: \"sigue sin hacer nada. Ademas, cuando guarde los cambios deberia volver al detalle de pedido\"\n\n\"Still does nothing. Also, when saving changes it should go back to the pedido detail\"\n\nMaybe the issue is simpler than I think. Maybe the Alert IS appearing, the mutations ARE running, the cache IS updating, but the UI doesn't reflect the changes because `setModoEdicion(false)` exits edit mode, and then the pedido data from `usePedidoDetalle` is stale/mock.\n\nWait, but I added `setQueryData` to update the cache! After `setQueryData`, the pedido in cache has the new items. So when `setModoEdicion(false)` exits edit mode, the UI should render the updated pedido from cache.\n\nUnless... the `usePedidoDetalle` query's refetch (triggered by `onSuccess` of the mutations) completes BEFORE the component re-renders after `setModoEdicion(false)`. If the refetch brings mock data that overwrites our optimistic update, then the user sees mock data.\n\nThe mutation's `onSuccess` calls `invalidateQueries`, which marks the query as stale. The next component render triggers a refetch. But when does `onSuccess` fire? It fires synchronously when `mutateAsync` resolves. Since `guardarCambios` uses `await` for each `mutateAsync`, the `onSuccess` fires before the next line in `guardarCambios`.\n\nWait, actually, `mutateAsync` resolves when the mutation succeeds. The `onSuccess` callback is part of the mutation promise chain but it doesn't block `mutateAsync` from resolving. Let me check TanStack Query docs...\n\nActually in TanStack Query v5, `mutateAsync` returns a promise that resolves with the mutation result. `onSuccess` is called as a side effect but the promise resolves before `onSuccess` completes? Or after?\n\nLooking at the TanStack Query source, `mutateAsync` returns the result after all callbacks (onSuccess, onSettled) have been called. So by the time `mutateAsync` resolves, `onSuccess` has ALREADY fired, which means `invalidateQueries` has already been called.\n\nSo the order is:\n1. `mutateAsync` resolves ÔåÆ `onSuccess` fires ÔåÆ `invalidateQueries(['pedido', id])` called\n2. But wait, `invalidateQueries` doesn't trigger an immediate refetch - it marks the query as stale\n3. Control returns to `guardarCambios`\n4. `queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', id], ...)` updates the cache\n5. `showToast`\n6. `setModoEdicion(false)`\n7. React re-renders ÔåÆ `usePedidoDetalle` sees stale query ÔåÆ triggers refetch\n8. Refetch tries API ÔåÆ 401 ÔåÆ mock data ÔåÆ cache updated with mock data\n9. React re-renders with mock data (overwrites our optimistic update!)\n\nYes! That's it! The order is:\n\n1. Mutation succeeds ÔåÆ `onSuccess` ÔåÆ `invalidateQueries` (marks stale)\n2. Our `setQueryData` updates cache with itemsEdit ÔåÉ this works\n3. `setModoEdicion(false)` ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ `usePedidoDetalle` sees stale query ÔåÆ refetch triggered\n4. Refetch completes (mock data) ÔåÆ cache overwritten with mock data\n5. Re-render with mock data\n\nSo the user briefly sees the updated data (from step 3) but then it gets overwritten by mock data (step 4-5).\n\nThe fix: I should move the `setQueryData` BEFORE the mutations (or use `onMutate` for optimistic update), and use `onSettled` for invalidation. OR I should NOT invalidate when in offline mode.\n\nActually, a simpler fix: move `setQueryData` to execute BEFORE the mutations start, so the cache is updated immediately. Then even if the mutations queue offline, the cache already has the changes.\n\nBut actually, the mutations' `onSuccess` will still invalidate, causing a refetch that overwrites the cache. So we need to also prevent the invalidation from overwriting our optimistic data.\n\nThe cleanest solution is to use `onSettled` instead of `onSuccess` for invalidation in the hooks, and let the screen handle `setQueryData`. But that changes the hooks...\n\nOK, let me think differently. What if I simply invalidate AFTER `setQueryData` and after `setModoEdicion(false)`? That way, the optimistic update is in place, then the refetch happens.\n\nBut the mutations' `onSuccess` still invalidates. So even if I add my own `queryClient.invalidateQueries` after `setQueryData`, the mutations' `onSuccess` already invalidated before our `setQueryData`.\n\nWait, but `invalidateQueries` just marks the query as stale. It doesn't trigger an immediate refetch. The refetch happens on the NEXT render cycle when `useQuery` sees the stale marker. So:\n\n1. Mutation resolves ÔåÆ `onSuccess` ÔåÆ invalidate (marks stale)\n2. Our `setQueryData` updates cache ÔåÉ sets new data\n3. `setModoEdicion(false)` ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ `usePedidoDetalle` sees stale ÔåÆ triggers refetch\n4. Refetch: API ÔåÆ 401 ÔåÆ mock data ÔåÆ cache updated\n5. Re-render with mock data\n\nSo even though we set the cache at step 2, the refetch at step 4 overwrites it.\n\nThe solution: We need to PREVENT the refetch from overwriting our optimistic data. We can do this by:\n\n1. **Cancelling the refetch**: `await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', id] })` before our `setQueryData`. But the refetch happens on the next render, not immediately.\n\n2. **Not invalidating**: Remove `invalidateQueries` from the mutation hooks' `onSuccess`. Instead, just use `setQueryData` in the screen. This is simple but changes the hooks.\n\n3. **Using `setQueryData` with the full pedido**: Instead of updating just `items`, update the full pedido object. The refetch will try to get fresh data. If it fails, the old data (which we set) remains. But the mock fallback in `usePedidoDetalle` means the refetch will SUCCEED with mock data.\n\n4. **Removing invalidate from offline cases**: Only invalidate if the API call succeeded, not in offline mode. But we can't distinguish in `onSuccess`.\n\n5. **Using `queryClient.setQueryDefaults` with a longer staleTime**: Not practical.\n\n6. **The real fix: use `onMutate` for optimistic updates and `onSettled` for invalidation in the hooks**. This is the proper TanStack Query pattern used by `useConfirmarEntrega` and `useCancelarPedido`.\n\nLooking at `useConfirmarEntrega`:\n```ts\nonMutate: async (pedidoId) => {\n  await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n  const previous = queryClient.getQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId]);\n  queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], (old) => {\n    if (!old) return old;\n    return { ...old, estado: 'ENTREGADO' as const };\n  });\n  return { previous };\n},\nonError: (err, pedidoId, context) => {\n  if (context?.previous) {\n    queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], context.previous);\n  }\n},\nonSettled: () => {\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\n`useConfirmarEntrega` uses `onMutate` + `onError`(rollback) + `onSettled`(invalidate). It does NOT have `onSuccess`. The `onMutate` updates the cache optimistically BEFORE the mutation runs. The `onSettled` invalidates AFTER the mutation (success or failure).\n\nBut in our case, the mutation hooks for items were built with a simpler pattern (only `onSuccess`). Let me think about what approach would work.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The user said \"sigue sin hacer nada\" - maybe the issue is even simpler than what I'm thinking. Maybe the mutations are timing out because the backend is unreachable.\n\nLet me ask a clarifying question: does the user see the Alert? Does \"Confirmar\" do anything? Is there an error? Since they said \"sigue sin hacer nada\" (still does nothing), maybe:\n\n1. The button tap doesn't trigger the Alert\n2. The Alert appears but nothing happens on \"Confirmar\"\n3. Something happens but there's no visible change\n\nAnd they also said \"cuando guarde los cambios deberia volver al detalle de pedido\" - when saving changes, should go back to the detalle de pedido. This implies that currently saving changes DOES exit edit mode (or should), and the user expects to see the detail view with the updated items.\n\nActually wait - maybe the user is saying that saving should navigate BACK (router.back()) like the confirmar/cancelar do:\n\n```ts\nconst handleConfirmar = () => {\n  confirmarMutation.mutate(id, {\n    onSuccess: () => {\n      showToast('Entrega confirmada', 'success');\n      router.back();  // <-- Goes back to list\n    },\n  });\n};\n```\n\nThe user might expect `guardarCambios` to also go back to the pedido list after saving. But they said \"volver al detalle de pedido\" (go back to pedido detail), not \"volver a la lista\" (go back to the list).\n\nHmm, actually \"volver al detalle de pedido\" might mean: after saving, switch from edit mode (modoEdicion) back to the read-only detalle view (which IS the same screen). And they want to see the updated items there.\n\nOK so the user expects:\n1. Save changes\n2. Exit edit mode\n3. See the detail view with updated items\n\nBut the problem is that after exit edit mode, the refetch brings mock data.\n\nLet me just apply the proper TanStack Query pattern. I'll:\n\n1. Remove the `setQueryData` from `guardarCambios` (since it gets overwritten)\n2. Add `onMutate` + `onSettled` pattern to the 3 item hooks, similar to `useConfirmarEntrega`\n\nActually wait, for the item hooks, `onMutate` is tricky because the mutationFn receives different data and we need to update the items array correctly.\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`:\n- `mutateAsync(itemId)` ÔåÆ onMutate receives `itemId`\n- Cancel queries, save previous data\n- `setQueryData(['pedido', pedidoId], old => ({ ...old, items: old.items.filter(i => i.id !== itemId) }))`\n- Return context with previous data\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n- `mutateAsync({ itemId, cantidad })` ÔåÆ onMutate receives `{ itemId, cantidad }`\n- But we don't have the full Item object, just itemId. We can't construct a proper PedidoItem.\n\nHmm, that's still the same issue. We don't have the full PedidoItem data in the mutation.\n\nLet me think about yet another approach. What if in `guardarCambios`, I:\n\n1. Set the cache optimistically FIRST (before any mutations)\n2. Cancel pending refetches\n3. Then run mutations\n4. After mutations complete, exit edit mode\n\nLike this:\n\n```ts\nconst guardarCambios = useCallback(() => {\n  if (!pedido) return;\n  \n  Alert.alert(..., async () => {\n    setGuardando(true);\n    try {\n      // Cancel any pending refetches to prevent overwrite\n      await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', id] });\n      \n      // Save previous data for rollback\n      const previousPedido = queryClient.getQueryData(['pedido', id]);\n      \n      // Set optimistic data FIRST\n      queryClient.setQueryData<Pedido>(['pedido', id], (old) => {\n        if (!old) return old;\n        return { ...old, items: itemsEdit };\n      });\n      \n      // Run mutations (will queue offline if fail)\n      // ...\n      \n      // Success - current cache is what we set + mutation onSuccess may invalidate\n      // But because we already set the cache, even if refetch fails, data is correct\n      \n      showToast('Items actualizados correctamente', 'success');\n      setModoEdicion(false);\n      setItemsEdit([]);\n    } catch (err) {\n      // Rollback on error\n      if (previousPedido) {\n        queryClient.setQueryData(['pedido', id], previousPedido);\n      }\n      const parsed = handleApiError(err);\n      showToast(parsed.message, 'error');\n    } finally {\n      setGuardando(false);\n    }\n  });\n}, [...]); \n```\n\nBut the mutations' `onSuccess` still calls `invalidateQueries`, which triggers a refetch on the next render. If the refetch succeeds (mock fallback), it overwrites our optimistic data.\n\nTo prevent this, I could also change the hooks to NOT invalidate, and instead have the screen handle invalidation. OR I could add a refetch that uses the mock data and see if it actually overwrites...\n\nWait, actually `cancelQueries` cancels any IN-FLIGHT queries. If a refetch is triggered later (due to invalidation), it would still run. `cancelQueries` only cancels currently running queries, not future ones.\n\nHmm, what if I use `queryClient.setQueryDefaults` or `queryClient.resetQueries` instead? No, that's not right either.\n\nOK, let me think about this from the TanStack Query perspective:\n\nWhen a query is invalidated (marked as stale), the next component that uses that query will trigger a refetch. The refetch goes through the `queryFn`. For `usePedidoDetalle`, `queryFn` tries the API first, and on failure falls back to mock data.\n\nThe refetch happens in the next microtask/render cycle, not immediately. So the timeline is:\n\n1. Mutation onSuccess ÔåÆ invalidate (query marked stale)\n2. Screen's setQueryData ÔåÆ cache updated with itemsEdit\n3. setModoEdicion(false) ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ usePedidoDetalle sees stale query ÔåÆ triggers refetch\n4. Microtask: refetch starts ÔåÆ API call\n5. API fails (401) ÔåÆ mock fallback\n6. Cache updated with mock data\n7. Re-render with mock data\n\nTo prevent step 6 from overwriting step 2, I need to ensure the refetch either doesn't happen or uses the same data.\n\nOptions:\nA. Remove `invalidateQueries` from hook onSuccess. Instead, invalidate from screen after a delay.\nB. Make the refetch use the same data (change usePedidoDetalle's queryFn to return cached data if available)\nC. Use `placeholderData: keepPreviousData` in usePedidoDetalle\nD. Don't invalidate pedido[id] - just invalidate pedidos list\n\nI think option A is the cleanest. Let me change the 3 item hooks to NOT invalidate the individual pedido query on success. Instead:\n\n1. Remove `onSuccess` from the hooks (or make it not invalidate ['pedido', pedidoId])\n2. In the screen, after `setQueryData`, invalidate only the PEDIDOS LIST (not the detail)\n\nThis way:\n1. Mutation succeeds ÔåÆ no invalidation of detail query\n2. Screen's setQueryData ÔåÆ cache updated with itemsEdit ÔåÉ this stays!\n3. setModoEdicion(false) ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ usePedidoDetalle sees fresh data (no stale marker)\n4. Screen manually invalidates pedidos list query\n5. User sees updated data\n\nThis is the cleanest fix. Let me implement it:\n\n1. In `usePedidos.ts` item hooks: change `onSuccess` to just invalidate `pedidos` and `reparto` (NOT `['pedido', pedidoId]`)\n2. In `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`: add `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] })` after setQueryData\n\nWait, but for the OTHER mutations (confirmarEntrega, cancelarPedido), they DO invalidate the individual pedido query. But those use `onMutate` pattern which updates the cache optimistically BEFORE the mutation runs, and `onSettled` which invalidates AFTER. For those, the optimistic update is in place before the invalidate triggers a refetch.\n\nThe key difference is that confirmar/cancelar use `onMutate` (pre-mutation) for cache update, while item hooks use `onSuccess` (post-mutation) for cache update. With `onMutate`, the cache is already updated when `onSettled` invalidates, so even if refetch fails, the correct data is displayed.\n\nSo the fix for item hooks should be:\n1. Remove `invalidateQueries(['pedido', pedidoId])` from `onSuccess`\n2. Only invalidate `['pedidos']` and `['reparto']`\n3. The screen's `setQueryData` handles updating the individual pedido cache\n\nOR even simpler: just keep the current approach but change the hooks to NOT invalidate the individual query. The screen handles updating the cache and the list queries.\n\nLet me implement this. I'll modify:\n1. `usePedidos.ts`: Remove `invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] })` from all 3 item hooks' onSuccess\n2. `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`: Keep the setQueryData, add `invalidateQueries(['pedidos'])` and `invalidateQueries(['reparto'])`\n\nWait, but currently the hooks ONLY invalidate `['pedido', pedidoId]` in onSuccess. They don't invalidate `['pedidos']` or `['reparto']`. So I need to add those too.\n\nActually, let me look at what the hooks do:\n\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', pedidoId] });\n},\n```\n\nThey only invalidate the individual pedido. They should also invalidate the list queries. Let me fix this properly.\n\nHere's my plan:\n\n**`usePedidos.ts`**: Change `onSuccess` in all 3 item hooks:\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  // Don't invalidate individual pedido to preserve optimistic cache update\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\n**`PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`**: In `guardarCambios`, keep the `setQueryData` and add:\n```ts\nqueryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedido', id] });\n```\n\nWait, that would cause the issue again. Let me think again...\n\nActually, the problem is that `invalidateQueries` marks the query as stale, causing a refetch on next render. The refetch overwrites our optimistic data with mock data.\n\nWhat if I use `queryClient.refetchQueries` instead? No, that does the same thing.\n\nWhat if I use `queryClient.setQueryData` AND THEN remove the stale marker?\n\nActually, `setQueryData` doesn't change the stale status. The query remains stale (from the invalidate). But if the query is stale, `useQuery` will trigger a refetch on the next render.\n\nWait, what if the `staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000` prevents the refetch? No, `invalidateQueries` overrides `staleTime` - it explicitly marks the query as stale, regardless of `staleTime`.\n\nOK, what if instead of invalidating the individual pedido query, I just set the data and don't invalidate? The list queries are invalidated (to show updated items in lists), but the individual pedido query keeps its fresh status (because we just set data). Since `staleTime` is 5 minutes, the query won't refetch for 5 minutes.\n\nSo the plan:\n1. In `guardarCambios`: `setQueryData` + `invalidateQueries(['pedidos'])` + `invalidateQueries(['reparto'])` - NO invalidate `['pedido', id]`\n2. In hooks: change `onSuccess` to NOT invalidate `['pedido', pedidoId]`. Only invalidate `['pedidos']` and `['reparto']`.\n\nWait but the hooks ONLY invalidate `['pedido', pedidoId]`. They don't touch `['pedidos']` or `['reparto']`. So I need to add those AND remove the individual one.\n\nHmm actually, this might break the normal case. If the API succeeds and items are updated on the server, we want the individual pedido query to refetch to get fresh data.\n\nBut `setQueryData` already puts the correct data in the cache. And the next time the user opens this pedido, it would be from cache (if within staleTime). If they want fresh data, they can pull-to-refresh.\n\nActually, let me think about this differently. The `guardarCambios` flow should:\n\n1. Update local state (itemsEdit) - already done by the user\n2. Call mutations - try API, queue offline if fails\n3. Update cache with itemsEdit - makes UI show changes\n4. Exit edit mode\n5. REFRESH from server if possible\n\nThe problem is step 5 overwrites step 3 when the API fails.\n\nWhat if I DON'T invalidate `['pedido', id]` in the `guardarCambios` flow, and instead rely on the mutation hooks to handle it? Since the mutation hooks now have `onSuccess` that invalidates, and the refetch would bring fresh data on API success...\n\nBut when API fails (offline), the hooks' `invalidateQueries` triggers a refetch with mock data...\n\nOK I have another idea. What if I change `usePedidoDetalle` to NOT use mock fallback? Then when the API fails, the query keeps its current data (our optimistic update).\n\nBut that would break other things - the initial load needs the mock fallback.\n\nWhat if instead I change the `staleTime` of `usePedidoDetalle` to a very long time (like Infinity) after a save? That's hacky.\n\nLet me try yet another approach: use `queryClient.setQueryData` with the `updatedAt` parameter to reset the stale status:\n\nActually, `setQueryData` doesn't affect the stale status. Only `staleTime` and `invalidateQueries` affect it.\n\nWhat if I use `queryClient.fetchQuery` to get the latest fresh data, and if it fails, swallow the error? That way, the cache keeps our optimistic data.\n\nOr better yet: what if I just DON'T invalidate from the screen, and DON'T trigger refetch? The refetch will happen naturally after `staleTime` expires (5 minutes) or when the user pulls to refresh.\n\nFor the hooks, I can change `onSuccess` to NOT invalidate `['pedido', pedidoId]` at all. Only invalidate `['pedidos']` and `['reparto']`. The individual pedido will be refetched when:\n- The user navigates to it fresh (new component mount)\n- `staleTime` expires\n- The user pulls to refresh\n\nThis is actually the correct TanStack Query pattern: invalidate list queries, not individual item queries, when the item is already in the cache.\n\nLet me implement this approach. I think it's clean and solves the problem.\n\nFor the hooks, I'll change `onSuccess`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  // Don't invalidate the single pedido to avoid overwriting optimistic cache\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nFor the screen, I'll keep `setQueryData` and `invalidateQueries(['pedidos'])` and `invalidateQueries(['reparto'])`.\n\nWait, but if the hooks ALREADY invalidate `['pedidos']` and `['reparto']` in `onSuccess`, the screen doesn't need to. But the hooks' `onSuccess` fires before we do `setQueryData`. So the invalidation happens BEFORE the cache update.\n\nHmm, that's fine. The invalidation marks the queries as stale. Our `setQueryData` updates the cache. The refetch for list queries will happen when the user navigates to a list screen. The refetch for the individual pedido WON'T happen because we didn't invalidate it.\n\nOK I think this is solid. Let me implement:\n\n1. `usePedidos.ts`: Change `onSuccess` for all 3 item hooks\n\nFor `useAgregarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nFor `useActualizarCantidadItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\nFor `useQuitarItem`:\n```ts\nonSuccess: () => {\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['pedidos'] });\n  queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['reparto'] });\n},\n```\n\n2. `PedidoDetalleScreen.tsx`: Keep the `setQueryData` in `guardarCambios`. The mutations' `onSuccess` will invalidate lists. The individual pedido won't refetch.\n\nWait, but there's still an issue. The `invalidateQueries` in the hooks fires when `onSuccess` fires, which is BEFORE our `setQueryData`. So:\n\n1. Mutation resolves ÔåÆ onSuccess ÔåÆ invalidateQueries(['pedidos']) ÔåÆ marks as stale\n2. Our setQueryData(['pedido', id], ...) updates pedido cache\n3. setModoEdicion(false) ÔåÆ re-render\n4. usePedidoDetalle sees query NOT stale (we didn't invalidate it) ÔåÆ no refetch ÔåÆ shows our optimistic data\n5. User navigates to pedidos list ÔåÆ sees stale marker ÔåÆ refetch ÔåÆ API ÔåÆ data\n\nThis works! The individual pedido detail shows the updated data (from our setQueryData), and the list queries will refetch when needed.\n\nLet me implement this.

### Mensaje:
Let me trace through the exact issue more carefully. After thinking through the whole flow:\n\nThe root cause is that the item mutation hooks' `onSuccess` calls `invalidateQueries(['pedido', pedidoId])`, which marks the individual pedido query as stale. On the next render cycle, `usePedidoDetalle` sees the stale marker and triggers a refetch. The refetch's queryFn tries the API (fails with 401) ÔåÆ falls back to mock data ÔåÆ overwrites the cache with mock data.\n\nEven though `guardarCambios` calls `setQueryData` AFTER the mutations, the invalidation already happened inside `onSuccess` (which fires before `mutateAsync` resolves back to `guardarCambios`). So:\n\n1. onSuccess ÔåÆ invalidate(['pedido', id]) ÔåÉ marks stale during mutation\n2. guardarCambios continues ÔåÆ setQueryData ÔåÉ updates cache\n3. setModoEdicion(false) ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ stale query ÔåÆ refetch ÔåÆ mock data ÔåÆ cache overwritten\n\nThe fix: Remove `invalidateQueries(['pedido', pedidoId])` from the 3 item hooks' onSuccess. Only invalidate list queries. The individual pedido cache is managed by the screen's `setQueryData`.\n\nLet me implement this.

### Mensaje:
Encontr├® la causa ra├¡z. Es un **race condition** con el refetch:\n\n1. Las mutaciones resuelven (offline) ÔåÆ `onSuccess` dispara `invalidateQueries(['pedido', id])` (marca como stale)\n2. `guardarCambios` hace `setQueryData` (cache actualizado)\n3. Sale del modo edici├│n ÔåÆ re-render ÔåÆ `usePedidoDetalle` ve query stale ÔåÆ **refetch** ÔåÆ API falla ÔåÆ mock data ÔåÆ **sobrescribe el cache**\n\nAunque `setQueryData` se ejecuta, el refetch posterior lo pisa con datos mock.\n\n### Soluci├│n\n\nSacar `invalidateQueries(['pedido', pedidoId])` de los 3 hooks de items. Solo invalidar las queries de listas. El pedido individual lo maneja el screen con `setQueryData`.

Those tests call `opts.mutationFn` and check `mockAddToQueue`. Since I didn't change the mutationFn, only the onSuccess, those tests should still pass.\n\nWait, do any tests check the onSuccess of these hooks? Let me check...\n\nLooking at the tests I added:\n```ts\ndescribe('useAgregarItem', () => {\n  it('encola en offlineStore cuando agregarItem falla', async () => {\n    mockAgregarItem.mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error('Network Error'));\n    vi.mocked(useMutation).mockImplementation((_options: unknown) => ({\n      mutate: vi.fn(), mutateAsync: vi.fn(), isPending: false, isError: false,\n      error: null, isSuccess: false, reset: vi.fn(), data: undefined,\n    } as never));\n    useAgregarItem('p1');\n    const opts = getMutationOptions();\n    const payload = { itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 };\n    const result = await opts.mutationFn(payload);\n    expect(mockAddToQueue).toHaveBeenCalledWith({\n      type: 'AGREGAR_ITEM',\n      payload: { pedidoId: 'p1', itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 },\n    });\n    expect(result).toEqual({ itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 });\n  });\n  it('llama a agregarItemRequest directamente cuando hay conexi├│n', async () => {\n    const responseData = { id: 'new-item', itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 };\n    mockAgregarItem.mockResolvedValueOnce(responseData);\n    vi.mocked(useMutation).mockImplementation((_options: unknown) => ({\n      mutate: vi.fn(), mutateAsync: vi.fn(), isPending: false, isError: false,\n      error: null, isSuccess: false, reset: vi.fn(), data: undefined,\n    } as never));\n    useAgregarItem('p1');\n    const opts = getMutationOptions();\n    const payload = { itemId: 'item-1', cantidad: 3 };\n    const result = await opts.mutationFn(payload);\n    expect(mockAddToQueue).not.toHaveBeenCalled();\n    expect(result).toEqual(responseData);\n  });\n});\n```\n\nThese tests only check `mutationFn`, not `onSuccess`. So they should still pass with my change.\n\nBut I should check if there are any tests that verify the onSuccess callback behavior (invalidations). Let me check the existing tests for `useConfirmarEntrega` and `useCancelarPedido` to see if they check `invalidateQueries`:\n\nLooking at the test file earlier (from memory), there are tests that check `mockInvalidateQueries` calls for confirmar/cancelar. The item hook tests didn't check `onSuccess`, they only checked `mutationFn`.\n\nSo the tests should still pass. Let me run them.\n\nWait, but I also need to update the `guardarCambios` to also invalidate `['pedidos']` and `['reparto']` since the hooks now only do it via `onSuccess` (which we might want to also do from the screen).
